coredata.dc:title: An efficient strategy for sensing pyrophosphate based on nitrogen-rich quantum dots combined with graphene oxide
coredata.dc:creator: Zou, Wu
Gong, Fuchun
Gu, Tingting
Cao, Zhong
Xia, Jiaoyun
coredata.dc:description: A facile approach was developed to prepare fluorescent nitrogen-rich quantum dots (NCQDs) using 2-azidoimidazole and ammonium hydroxide as reactants. The nitrogen and carbon elements of NCQDs form the frameworks of their nanostructures together and bear multiple active sites. By using NCQDs as fluorophores and graphene oxide (GO) as controllers, a hydrogen binding-based nanosensor was constructed for the detection of pyrophosphate (PPi). In NCQDs-GO sensing assemblies, the binding of GO to NCQDs results in the fluorescence quenching by FRET mechanism; while the addition of PPi leads to the fluorescence recovery of NCQDs because of the competitive binding between NCQDs and PPi to GO. The fluorescence intensity showed linearity with the added PPi concentration ranging from 0 to 400 μM with the detection limit of 8.3 nM (σ = 3). This procedure for the fabrication of amino-functionalized carbon quantum dots is simpler and more efficient compared to conventional doping and surface modification methods. The proposed NCQDs-GO sensing assemblies showed high selectivity and sensitivity to PPi over the competing anions in aqueous solution.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-rich quantum dots
Synthesis
Nanosensors
Pyrophosphate detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Construction of nanosensors and detection of PPi
Construction of fluorescent nanoprobes for PPi
Selectivity and anti-interference performance of NCQDs-GO assemblies
Primary applications of the nanoprobes for PPi

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM micrographs at different magnifications (a, b). Inset in b shows the corresponding NCQDs size distribution histogram. (c) XPS survey and (d) deconvoluted spectra of N1s in the NCQDs.
(a) SEM image of GO and (b) NCQDs-GO; (c) FT-IR spectrum and (d) Raman spectrum of GO, NCQDs-GO and NCQDs.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of NCQDs with the addition of GO and the subsequent treatment with PPi; Inset in (a): The digital pictures of NCQDs and NCQDs-GO and NCQDs-GO with the addition of PPi under UV light; (b and c) relationship between the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs-GO at 432 nm and the concentration of PPi; (d) relationship between the absorbance intensity at 320 nm of NCQDs-GO and the concentration of PPi.

(a) Fluorescence response of the as-prepared NCQDs-GO dyads to PPi and various interfering materials. (b) Fluorescence response of the NCQDs-GO dyads to PPi in the presence of various interfering species. The concentrations of PPi and interfering species are 10 μM and 30 μM, respectively.
(a) Synthesis strategy of the NCQDs using 2-azidoimidazole and ammonia; (b) schematic illustration of PPi detection principle based on the NCQDs combined with GO.
Determination of PPi in rabbit serum and plasma using NCQDs-GO.

head.ce:title: An efficient strategy for sensing pyrophosphate based on nitrogen-rich quantum dots combined with graphene oxide

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Biologic Engineering, Changsha University of Science and Technology, Changsha 410114, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Biologic Engineering
Changsha University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Materials Protection for Electric Power and Transportation, Changsha University of Science and Technology, Changsha 410114, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Materials Protection for Electric Power and Transportation
Changsha University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile approach was developed to prepare fluorescent nitrogen-rich quantum dots (NCQDs) using 2-azidoimidazole and ammonium hydroxide as reactants. The nitrogen and carbon elements of NCQDs form the frameworks of their nanostructures together and bear multiple active sites. By using NCQDs as fluorophores and graphene oxide (GO) as controllers, a hydrogen binding-based nanosensor was constructed for the detection of pyrophosphate (PPi). In NCQDs-GO sensing assemblies, the binding of GO to NCQDs results in the fluorescence quenching by FRET mechanism; while the addition of PPi leads to the fluorescence recovery of NCQDs because of the competitive binding between NCQDs and PPi to GO. The fluorescence intensity showed linearity with the added PPi concentration ranging from 0 to 400 μM with the detection limit of 8.3 nM (σ = 3). This procedure for the fabrication of amino-functionalized carbon quantum dots is simpler and more efficient compared to conventional doping and surface modification methods. The proposed NCQDs-GO sensing assemblies showed high selectivity and sensitivity to PPi over the competing anions in aqueous solution.

A facile and efficient strategy for the preparation of amino nitrogen‑carbon quantum dots (NRQDs) was developed. A highly selective sensor was then constructed for detecting PPi in rabbit serum and plasma samples using NRQDs combined with graphene oxide (GO).
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: The development of highly selective and sensitive sensors for the detection of pyrophosphate anion (PPi) has attracted increasing attention in recent years because of its significance in the field of biology, environment and clinic diagnostics [1–3]. However, it is a formidable challenge to construct PPi receptors based on hydrogen bonding interaction due to the high solvation energy of PPi in water and the presence of other competitive anions in aqueous medium [4–6]. As for PPi recognition, the common strategy is the use of metal-organic complexes or positively charged species as receptors. Owing to the strong interaction of PPi with metal complexes or charged compounds, the fluorescent signal of the receptors is altered and PPi detection is realized. Currently, a large number of the reported metal mediated-sensors are available [7–9], however, these methods typically suffer from several limitations, including unsatisfactory selectivity, low signal intensities, high photobleaching and low water-solubility of the small organic molecule-based PPi receptors. Thus, it is of tremendous interest and importance to analytical chemists to develop a new selectively and sensitively fluorescent sensors for the detection of PPi.

As a nanofluorophore, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) can provide us with a more favorable choice to design excellent sensors for PPi detection and bioimaging [10–12]. CQDs may be endowed with better characteristics than the classical small organic fluorophores, including high surface area, special optical, electronic performance, favorable water solubility and biocompatibility [13,14]. CQDs can be achieved from a facile, cost-effective and environmentally friendly combustion/thermal approach and the as-prepared CQDs are also further functionalized by doping or surface modification with carboxylic, carbonyl or amino moieties on the particle surfaces, to which metal ions or anions can bind [15–17]. By using various modified CQDs as receptors, some highly sensitive, water-soluble and biocompatible PPi nanosensors have been developed [18,19]. Unfortunately, similar to small organic dye-based sensors, most of the reported PPi nanosensors to date are based on the coordination of metal-nanoreceptors with PPi, and their unsatisfactory selectivity remains a hamper for the practical application in aqueous solution.

As recently shown, the doped CQDs with nitrogen can dramatically increase quantum yield and offer more active sites because of the electron-withdrawing ability of nitrogen atoms [20–22]. Several methods, such as the treatment of carbon compounds with ammonia at high temperatures, carbonization of nitrogen containing compounds(NCCs) at high temperature, liquid phase polymerization of NCCs or chemical vapor deposition with nitrogen containing species have been developed for the preparation of various doped CQDs with improved properties [23–26]. However, these methods suffer from a common drawback is that most of the nitrogen atoms were grafted into the CQDs under harsh conditions and contain relatively less content of nitrogen [27,28], which may limit their wide applications. Recently, a new class of quantum dots, nitrogen-rich quantum dots (NCQDs) attract our interest, in which the carbon and nitrogen atoms form the framework of their nanostructures together [29–31].Compared to the doped CQDs with nitrogen atoms, NCQDs contain higher percentage of nitrogen content and more active sites, and can effectively tune their chemical selectivity and optical properties, which is extremely important for the design of the sensitive and selective nanosensors.

Herein, we developed a facile strategy for the fabrication of NCQDs using 2-azidoimidazole and aqueous ammonia as reactants. NCQDs contain abundant amino groups, which exhibit good water-solubility and high fluorescence quantum yield. By using multiple hydrogen binding interaction sites as recognition units, we actively integrate NCQDs with graphene oxides (GO) and thus construct a nanosensor for the selective and sensitive detection of PPi. As illustrated in Scheme 1b, NCQDs are employed as receptors, and GOs as signal controllers that can adsorb the NCQDs and result in the fluorescence quenching of NCQDs. When PPi is added into the NCQDs-GO solution, a competitive interaction with GO existed between PPi and NCQDs, which disturbed the binding between GO and NCQDs. It was then possible to detach the NCQDs from the surface of GO because of the strong multiple hydrogen-binding interaction of PPi with NCQDs, and thus the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs could be recovered dramatically.

2-Aminoimidazole and the other chemicals were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China) and were used as received. Rabbit serum and plasma samples were provided by Xiang Ya School of Medicine, Central South University (Changsha, China). The stock solutions (20 mM) of interfering species including F−, Cl−, Br−, H2PO4 −, CH3COO−, HSO4 −, NO3 −, SCN−, SO4 2 −, HCO3 −, CO3 2−, HPO4 2 −, S2−, SO3 2−, AMP, ADP, ATP, Na+ and K+ were prepared by dissolving their compounds in ultrapure water, and stock solution of NCQDs and sensor NCQDs-GO for PPi detection (0.5 mM) was prepared in HEPES buffer (10 mM, pH 7.4).

UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a UV–vis 2600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence measurements were carried out on F-7000 Spectrofluorometers (Shimadzu, Japan). 1HNMR spectra were recorded with the Bruker AVB-400 MHz NMR spectrometer (Bruker biospin, Switzerland). Scanning electron microscopic (SEM) images were recorded with a PHILIPS TECNAI 10 SEM instrument (Philips, Netherlands). Germany). The pH measurements were performed with a pH-meter PB-10 (Sartorius, Germany).

An appropriate amount of 2-aminoimidazole (6.68 g, 80 mM) was added into a 80.0 mL HCl aqueous solution (6.0 M) and stirred under ice-water bath. After dissolution, a 50.0 mL NaNO2 solution (6.92 g, 100 mM) was slowly dropped into the resulting 2-aminoimidazole solution. The mixture was reacted for an additional 45 min. With strong stirring, a 50 mL NaN3 solution (6.52 g, 100 mM) was added dropwise, and the stirring was continued for 1.5 h. The resulting mixture was then poured into an ice–water mixture. After adjusting the pH value of them to 7.0–8.0 with NaHCO3, the mixture was extracted 3 times by ethylacetate. The combined organic phase was dried over anhydrous Na2SO4. After filtration and evaporation, the solid residue was purified by silica gel flash column chromatography (n-hexane: ethyl acetate = 1:1, v/v), yielding 8.07 g of a light yellow solid. Yield: 93.1%. 1HNMR (500 MHz, DMSO‑d 6, δ): 6.88 (s, 2H), 12.03 (s, 1H).

2-Azidoimidazole (1.0 g), 12 mL aqueous ammonia (25%) and 36 mL tetrahydrofuran were added into a flask. The mixture was heated to 75 °C and reacted for 2 h. After recovery of the solvent, the dark brown aqueous phase was collected by removing the large dots through centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 45 min. The resulting solution was then dialyzing against ultrapure water through a dialysis membrane (3500 kD) for 48 h. Finally, the dialysate was filtrated with a 0.2 μm filter and dried under vacuum-freeze, giving cinnamon-colored solid residue.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of nanosensors and detection of PPi
ce:section.ce:para: An appropriate amount of NCQDs solution (5.0 mL, 32.0 μg·mL−1) and 1.0 mL of GO solution (5.0 μg·mL−1) were added to a 10 mL cuvette, the NCQDs-GO assemblies formed after a 5 min reaction. For the PPi detection, the resulted NCQDs-GO assemblies and appropriate aliquot of PPi solution were transferred into a tube. The PPi standard solution with different concentrations were diluted with a HEPES buffer solution (10 mM, pH 7.4) containing 50 mmol·L−1 NaCl, 10 mmol·L−1 KCl, 3.5 mmol·L−1 MgCl2, and then added 25 μL PPi into the NCQDs-GO assembly solution (100 μL) after reaction for 5 min at room temperature. In a typical experiment, test solutions were prepared by diluting the stock solutions with HEPES buffer at pH 7.4. Fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded after the addition of analytes at room temperature for 5 min. The fluorescence intensity was recorded at 432 nm with the excitation wavelength of 360 nm, and the slit widths of emission and excitation were fixed at 5 nm. The detection approaches for other interferences including different anions were similar to that of PPi. When actual samples were determined, the PPi standard solution was substituted by the prepared rabbit serum and plasma samples. Each experiment was repeated three times and took the average.

Fluorescent NRQDs with high percentage of nitrogen atoms were synthesized using 2-azidoimidazole and ammonia as reactants by a mixed solvothermal method. As depicted in Scheme 1a, the NRQDs were prepared by refluxing 2-azidoimidazole in an ammonia water-tetrahydrofuran system, where the formation of nanoparticles, surface passivation and functionalization were accomplished simultaneously. As shown in Fig. 1a, b, the typical TEM images indicate that the NCQDs are well dispersed with an average size of 5 nm. The full scan XPS spectrum of the NCQDs showed that C1s, N1s, and O1s peaks appeared at 286.1, 399.7, and 532.3 eV, respectively, and the typical XPS surveys of the as-prepared a-NCQDs indicate a high nitrogen content(40.25%), which is consistent with the results of their element analysis (Fig. 1c; Table S4, ESI†). The deconvoluted N1s XPS spectrum of the a-NCQDs around 398.35 eV, 399.24 eV, 399.87 eV and 400.70 eV were pointed to the nitrogen atom of CN, NH2, CNC and NC3, respectively (Fig. 1d). The infrared (IR) spectrum of NCQDs is given in Fig. S1c, ESI†. The peak at 3425 cm−1 indicates the existence of amino or hydroxyl groups, while the peak at 1462 cm−1 is due to the CN stretching mode, and the peaks at 1238 cm−1 correspond to the CN groups [31]. These data demonstrate that the as-prepared NCQDs were surrounded by -NH2 groups. In addition, the peak at 250–325 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectrum of NCQDs represents a typical absorption of imidazolyl systems (Fig. S1b, ESI†).

To further evaluate the optical properties of the NCQDs, the detailed fluorescence experiments were carried out using different excitation wavelengths (Fig. S1a, ESI†): as the Ex increases, the E
m peak shifts to longer wavelength and the intensity decreases, which shows the typical fluorescence characteristics of quantum dots. Using quinine sulfate as a standard, the fluorescence quantum yield of NCQDs was found to be 35.8% (Table S2, ESI†). Conventional methods for the fabrication of amino-functionalized quantum dots require the process of nanoparticle synthesis, passivation and surface modification with nitrogen containing species. And these nanostructures are often changed in grain structure, optical and electric properties due to the decoration. The obvious advantage of the present mixed-solvothermal method over previous method is that it can dissolve reactants (2-azidoimidazole), which give more homogeneous quantum dots [32]. This method integrates the formation of nanoparticles, surface passivation and functionalization, which is simpler and more efficient compared to conventional surface modification or doping for the amino-functionalization of carbon quantum dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of fluorescent nanoprobes for PPi
ce:section.ce:para: To examine the sensing ability of NCQDs toward PPi, we constructed a sensor based on NCQDs coupling with GO. The formation of the assemblies of NCQDs and GO was confirmed from the SEM micrographs as shown in Fig. 2b. The SEM image clearly reveal that NCQDs are binding onto GO and lead to the morphological change of GO. The attachment of NCQDs on the GO was also tested by IR spectroscopy. From the curve c of Fig. 2, the peak at 1646 cm−1 is due to carbonyl groups, while the peak at 1478 cm−1 indicates the existence of -NH groups. The peak at 2930 cm−1 corresponds to the –NH stretching mode. Raman spectra show also that NCQDs are binding to GO (Fig. 2d).

The fluorescence spectra of NCQDs, NCQDs + GO and NCQDs + GO + PPi indicated that the fluorescence quenching of NCQDs in the presence of GO and recovery of NCQDs + GO with the subsequent addition of PPi (Fig. 3a). The digital pictures of NCQDs, NCQDs-GO and NCQDs-GO with the addition of PPi under UV light consistent with this result (Inset). Under the optimized conditions (Fig. S2, ESI†), the fluorescence shows continuous increase with the addition of PPi (Fig. 3b). These results showed that NCQDs were stably confined on the GO surface and may form hydrogen bonds with PPi detaching from GO surface. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity showed linearity with the added PPi concentration ranging from 0 to 400 μM (Fig. 3c). The detection limit was calculated to be 8.3 nM (σ = 3), which is lower than reported method based on small organic molecule-based PPi receptors [33, 34]. UV–vis titrations are consistent with the results of fluorescence spectra above (Fig. 3d).

The plausible sensing mechanism is depicted in Scheme 1b. In the NCQDs-GO sensing assemblies, the NCQDs with abundant amino and imine groups are binding to the GO through noncovalent bond interaction, resulting in efficient quenching of their fluorescence by FRET mechanism, that is, upon addition of NCQDs into the GO solution, the NCQDs are prone to get close to the surface of GO through the polar groups interaction. After adding PPi to the sensing system, the NCQDs desorbed from the surface of GO because of the formation of hydrogen bonds between NCQDs and PPi, and thereby lead to remarkable recovery of the fluorescence of NCQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference performance of NCQDs-GO assemblies
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the selectivity and anti-disturbance capacity of NCQDs-GO assemblies toward PPi, various interfering species including biologically relevant molecules, metal ions and anions were tested. Upon the addition of different anions and nucleotides, only PPi can induce fluorescence intensity increase at 432 nm of the NCQDs-GO nanocomposites (Fig. 4a), other representative anions and nucleotides, such as F−, Cl−, Br−, H2PO4 −, CH3COO−, HSO4 −, NO3 −, SCN−, SO4 2 −, HCO3 −, CO3 2−, HPO4 2 −, S2−, SO3 2− AMP, ADP, ATP, Na+ and K+ showed almost no effects on the emission behavior of NCQDs-GO. The competition experiments were also carried out in the presence of NCQDs-GO and PPi mixed with many anions and nucleotides (F−, Cl−, Br−, H2PO4 −, CH3COO−, HSO4 −, NO3 −, SCN−, SO4 2 −, HCO3 −, CO3 2−, HPO4 2 −, S2−, SO3 2−, AMP, ADP, ATP, Na+, and K+), respectively (Fig. 4b). No significant fluorescence change was found by comparison with that without the other anions and nucleotides, which revealed strong anti-disturbance capacity of the NCQDs-GO toward PPi over other common anions and nucleotides. Taken together, all these results demonstrate that our NCQDs-GO nanocomposite sensors are highly selective to PPi. We speculate that the multiple hydrogen bonding of NCQDs to PPi make them highly selective.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Primary applications of the nanoprobes for PPi
ce:section.ce:para: Subsequently, we investigated the analytical performance of the NCQDs-GO sensors for PPi detection in complicated biological samples. The sensing capability of the NCQDs-GO dyads-based sensors was evaluated by quantitative determination of PPi in rabbit serum and plasma. The recoveries of the spiked samples range from 95.7% to 105.8%, indicating the practicability of the proposed sensors NCQDs-GO (Table 1 ). These results suggest that our strategy for the construction of PPi sensors has an obvious advantage in selectivity.

In summary, we presented a facile methodology for the fabrication of fluorescent NCQDs using 2-azidoimidazole and ammonia as reactants. The NCQDs bear high percentage of the element nitrogen, endowed with high fluorescence quantum yield, multiple hydrogen binding interaction sites and good water-solubility. By coupling NCQDs with GO, a novel hydrogen binding interaction-based sensor for PPi detection was constructed, which shows excellent selectivity and sensitivity to PPi in aqueous solution. Therefore, the present strategy for the fabrication of amino-functionalized carbon quantum dots may offer a new sight for the preparation of functionalized-nanomaterials. The NCQDs-based receptors can provide an accurate method for the detection of PPi in complex biological samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 31527803 and 21545010) and the Natural Science Foundation of State Key Laboratory of Life Organic Chemistry, Shanghai Institute of Organic Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences (No. SKLBNPC17344).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2018.06.004.
